title.rendered: Buying holiday money in uncertain times. What you should think about this year

content.rendered:
If you’ve booked a holiday and are keeping half an eye on exchange rates, you may have seen this

recent headline in the Telegraph: “Buy your holiday money now. The pound is in for a rocky year.”
(You can read the original article here.)

At Travel FX, we speak to people like you every day who are simply trying to answer one question: how do I avoid nasty surprises when I’m abroad?

This article isn’t financial advice and it’s not about predicting the markets. It’s about helping you think through your options, so you can choose what feels right for your trip.

Why exchange rate headlines matter to you

You don’t need to follow the financial pages to feel the impact of currency movements. When the pound weakens, even slightly, it can mean:

  • your spending money doesn’t stretch as far

  • everyday holiday costs feel higher than expected

  • you’re left wishing you’d planned a bit more ahead

We saw this last year when the pound moved around noticeably over short periods. If you had already sorted your travel money, you were largely insulated from those swings. If you waited, you may have ended up paying more for the same amount of currency.

There’s no right or wrong decision, but uncertainty tends to hit hardest when things are left until the last minute.

Buying travel money early is about certainty, not predictions

It’s worth being clear. Nobody knows what the pound will do next. Exchange rates move up and down, often without warning.

For many people, buying travel money earlier isn’t about trying to “beat the market.” It’s about:

  • knowing what your holiday money will cost

  • avoiding a last-minute rush before you travel

  • taking one more thing off your pre-holiday checklist

Think of it like booking flights. You don’t wait until the night before in the hope prices might drop. You book when you’re comfortable with the price and move on.

What people like you often choose to do

From our experience, many customers take a simple, practical approach:

  • buying some or all of their travel money once their holiday is booked

  • spreading purchases if they don’t want to commit all at once

  • making sure funds are sorted before travelling, rather than relying on airport exchange desks

It’s less about perfect timing and more about feeling organised and in control.

Cash, cards or a combination?

Most people now travel with a mix. Cards are convenient for hotels and larger payments, while cash is still useful for taxis, tips and smaller day-to-day spends.

Whatever you prefer, planning ahead gives you more choice and usually better value than leaving everything until you arrive or pass through the airport.

Important note

This article is intended to help you think about practical travel money options. It is not financial advice and it does not make predictions about future exchange rate movements. Any decision about if or when to buy travel money should be based on your own circumstances and comfort level.

A gentle reminder from Travel FX

If exchange rate headlines are making you feel uneasy, it may be worth thinking ahead. Sorting your travel money early can mean one less thing to worry about and more confidence in your holiday budget.

With straightforward online ordering and secure home delivery, Travel FX makes this part simple, so you can focus on enjoying your trip.

Bottom line: you can’t control the pound, but you can control how prepared you feel before you travel. For many people, that peace of mind is worth planning for.

@graph.name: Travel FX Blog
@graph.description: Travel money news and tips from Travel FX

@graph.name: Lucy Reed
@graph.description: Hi! I’m Lucy, as well as writing blog posts I lead Customer Experience and UX at Travel FX. With over 15 years’ experience in the foreign exchange industry, I specialise in helping UK travellers understand exchange rates, avoid unnecessary fees, and feel confident about their travel money before they fly. Having lived both in the UK and overseas, and travelled extensively over the years, I understand first-hand how important it is to get the details right when you’re planning a trip. From managing your travel budget to choosing the right foreign currency or travel money card, small decisions can make a big difference.
